labels: E001: AI failure plan for security breaches
E001.1: Assign breach response lead and document incident response procedures.
E001.2: Define procedures for customer communications, regulatory reporting, and vendor notifications.
E001.3: Implement system freeze capabilities, vulnerability fixes, access control updates.
E001.4: Establish guidance on preserving evidence for legal review.
E002: AI failure plan for harmful outputs
E002.1: Implementing customer communication protocols and immediate mitigation steps.
E002.2: Defining harmful output category definitions and external support engagement.
E003: AI failure plan for hallucinations
E003.1: Establishing compensation assessment procedures and remediation measures.
E003.2: Defining hallucination incident types and external support coordination.
E004.1: Documentation defining which AI system changes require approval with assigned leads, plus approval records.
E004.2: Implementing code signing and verification processes for AI models and deployment artifacts.
E005: Assess cloud vs on-prem processing
E005.1: Risk assessment and decision record evaluating cloud vs. on-premises factors.
E006.1: Vendor assessment records showing evaluation criteria, scoring, verification, and approval decisions.
E008.1: Quarterly reviews of decision processes including AI system changes, foundational model selection.
E008.2: Collect and implement external feedback on AI systems.
E009.1: Configuring logging for third-party interactions with captured metadata.
E010: Establish AI acceptable use policy
E010.1: Policy document defining acceptable and prohibited AI usage.
E010.2: Implementation of detection and monitoring systems for AUP violations.
E010.3: User-facing alerts when acceptable use policy is violated.
E010.4: Real-time blocking/alerting capabilities and violation tracking.
E011.1: Maintaining AI infrastructure location documentation.
E012.1: Identify applicable regulations, document compliance procedures, and review every 6 months.
E013.1: Defining quality objectives, metrics, and risk management approach.
E013.2: Establishing change management, approval processes.
E013.3: Implementing defect tracking, continuous improvement, and post-market monitoring.
E013.4: Establishing data management and record-keeping systems.
E013.5: Documenting communication procedures with regulatory authorities.
E015.1: Capture system activity details including inputs, processing steps, outputs, and metadata.
E015.2: Implement storage with retention policies, access controls, and data sanitation.
E015.3: Ensure logs are tamper-evident and independently verifiable.
E016.1: Display clear notices when users interact with AI chatbots.
E016.2: Provide audio notifications at the beginning of voice calls.
E016.3: Mark AI-generated content with metadata, watermarks, or labels.
E016.4: Notify users when autonomous AI agents perform actions.
E016.5: Systems respond to user questions about whether they are interacting with AI.
E017.1: Policy document defining transparency documentation requirements.
E017.2: Transparency documentation artifacts: model cards, datasheets, interpretability reports, AI Bill of Materials.
E017.3: Policy defining transparency sharing practices with stakeholder categories.
A001.1: Define and communicate input data usage policies including how customer data is used for inference and model training, e
A001.2: Implement technical controls to enforce data retention and deletion policies through automating data deletion based on r
A001.3: Document processes for handling end-user data subject rights including opt-in/opt-out rights, access, portability, or de
A002.1: Establishing output ownership and usage rights policies, specifying customer ownership of AI-generated outputs, defining
A003: Limit AI agent data collection
A003.1: Configuring data collection limits to reduce data and privacy exposure, limiting data collection to task-relevant inform
A003.2: Deploying monitoring mechanisms ensuring AI systems only perform necessary inference and logging deviations from defined
A003.3: Integrating with existing authorization systems to align agent access permissions with organizational policies.
A004.1: Providing user guidance on protecting confidential information.
A004.2: Leveraging foundation model provider protections with zero data retention policies.
A004.3: Implementing technical controls to detect proprietary information in outputs.
A004.4: Establishing output monitoring for high-risk IP scenarios.
A005.1: Establishing explicit consent and disclosure for combined data usage.
A005.2: Implementing customer data isolation controls with strict logical and physical separation.
A005.3: Implementing privacy-enhancing technologies to reduce competitive exposure.
A006.1: Implementing safeguards to prevent personal data leakage through AI system outputs and logs, filtering prompts and outpu
A006.2: Requiring authentication and authorization for PII access with role-based access controls.
A006.3: Integrating with existing data loss prevention (DLP) systems to monitor and block outputs containing personal data.
A007.1: Documenting foundation model provider IP protections including indemnification clauses.
A007.2: Establishing supplementary content filtering mechanisms for IP screening.
A007.3: Implementing user guidance and guardrails to reduce IP risk.
D001.1: Implementing factual accuracy controls via fact-checking mechanisms, flagging uncertain responses.
D001.2: Requiring citations for factual claims, implementing source reliability checks.
D001.3: Maintaining uncertainty communication through confidence levels and disclaimers.
D002.1: Third-party evaluation report showing hallucination testing results.
D003.1: Function call validation and authorization, restricting tool access to approved functions, validating parameters before
D003.2: Enforcing rate limits and transaction caps for autonomous tool use.
D003.3: Execution monitoring and logging; tracking all tool calls, monitoring for unauthorized access attempts.
D003.4: Requiring human approval for sensitive tool operations.
D003.5: Reviewing patterns of AI tool usage; identifying anomalies, updating tool permissions.
D004: Third-party testing of tool calls
D004.1: Third-party evaluation report showing tool call testing results.
C001.1: Internal policy document or risk framework defining AI risk categories with severity levels and examples.
C001.2: Quarterly reviews of risk taxonomy with review dates, participants, decisions, and version history.
C002.1: Conducting pre-deployment testing with documented results, risk assessments, and approval sign-offs.
C002.2: Integrating AI system testing into established SDLC gates.
C002.3: Implementing pre-deployment vulnerability scanning of AI artifacts and dependencies.
C003.1: Detecting and blocking harmful output types through content filtering systems.
C003.2: Restricting high-risk recommendations in sensitive domains with disclaimers.
C003.3: Monitoring for discriminatory patterns and implementing fairness checks.
C003.4: Evaluating harm mitigation controls using performance metrics.
C004.1: Blocking rules, defensive prompting, or filtering for out-of-scope requests.
C004.2: Logs showing out-of-scope attempts with frequency data and boundary updates.
C004.3: User-facing guidance explaining system capabilities and limitations.
C005: Prevent customer-defined high risk outputs
C005.1: Implementing detection and blocking mechanisms aligned with organizational risk taxonomy.
C005.2: Establishing escalation procedures for flagged high-risk content.
C006.1: Establishing output sanitization and validation procedures before presenting content to users.
C006.2: Implementing security labeling and content handling based on trust level.
C006.3: Detecting advanced output-based attack patterns.
C007.1: Document or policy defining high-risk outputs requiring human review.
C007.2: Detection code, configuration, or rules engine showing high-risk output filtering.
C007.3: Workflow documentation for human review process for flagged outputs.
C008.1: Establish ongoing monitoring of AI outputs across risk categories through regular evaluations.
C008.2: Maintain documentation recording identified risk scenarios with examples.
C008.4: Integrate AI output monitoring with existing security tools.
C009: Enable real-time feedback and intervention
C009.1: Enable user intervention capabilities such as pause, stop, or redirect system behavior.
C009.2: Review user feedback and intervention logs regularly.
C010: Third-party testing for harmful outputs
C010.1: Third-party evaluation report showing harmful output testing.
C011: Third-party testing for out-of-scope outputs
C011.1: Third-party evaluation report showing out-of-scope output testing.
C012: Third-party testing for customer-defined risk
C012.1: Third-party evaluation report showing testing of customer-defined risk.
B001: Third-party testing of adversarial robustness
B001.1: Third-party evaluation report demonstrating adversarial robustness testing including risk taxonomy, methodology, finding
B001.2: Penetration test reports incorporating AI-specific test cases, shared threat models, and testing calendars.
B002.1: Implementing monitoring for prompt injection patterns, jailbreak techniques, adversarial input attempts.
B002.2: Logging suspected adversarial attacks with relevant context and escalating based on severity.
B002.3: Updating detection rules based on emerging adversarial techniques quarterly.
B002.4: Implementing adversarial input detection prior to AI model processing.
B002.5: Integrating adversarial input detection into existing security operations tooling.
B003: Manage public release of technical details
B003.1: Policy document defining limitations and approval requirements for publicly sharing AI system technical details.
B003.2: Approval records for public AI communications.
B004.1: Implementing systems distinguishing between high-volume legitimate usage and adversarial behavior.
B004.2: Implementing rate limiting and query restrictions.
B004.3: Conducting simulated external attack testing of AI endpoints.
B004.4: Tracking identified vulnerabilities and implementing protective measures.
B005.1: Integration of automated moderation tools to filter inputs before reaching the foundation model.
B005.2: Documentation explaining moderation approach including tool selection rationale and threshold settings.
B005.3: User-facing communication when inputs are blocked.
B005.4: Logging system capturing flagged inputs with metadata tracking.
B005.5: Analysis of filter performance metrics with documented threshold adjustments.
B006: Prevent unauthorized AI agent actions
B006.1: Restricting agent access to approved backend services and APIs, enforcing network segmentation or API gateway rules.
B006.2: Deploying monitoring and alerting for agent actions that exceed security boundaries.
B007: Enforce user access privileges to AI systems
B007.1: System-level access controls tailored to AI systems using role-based or attribute-based access.
B007.2: Conducting access reviews and updates at least quarterly.
B008.1: Restricting access to production AI models based on job function.
B008.2: Applying scoped API tokens, TLS for all endpoint traffic, schema validation.
B008.3: Securing model hosting environments with minimal container images.
B008.4: Verifying model integrity before deployment using cryptographic checksums.
B009.1: Reducing or limiting the number of results shown in outputs to relevant only.
B009.2: Providing user-facing notices about output limitations.
B009.3: Limiting the fidelity of model outputs with rounding, threshold bands, or obfuscation.
F001.1: Provider model cards and documentation describing offensive cyber capabilities and mitigation strategies.
F001.2: Deploy content filtering to detect requests for malicious code generation and vulnerability exploitation.
F002.1: List of foundation models with CBRN capability information and risk evaluations.
F002.2: Monitoring dashboard or alert configuration for catastrophic misuse patterns.
COV: Covert Channels in Model Outputs
EDH-I: Excessive Data Handling During Inference

GOVERN-1.1: Legal and regulatory requirements involving AI are understood, managed, and documented.
GOVERN-1.2: Trustworthy AI characteristics integrated into organizational policies, processes, and procedures.
GOVERN-1.3: Processes and procedures in place to determine needed level of risk management activities based on organizational risk t
GOVERN-1.4: Risk management process and outcomes established through transparent policies, procedures, and controls based on organiz
GOVERN-1.5: Ongoing monitoring and periodic review of risk management process and outcomes; roles and responsibilities defined.
GOVERN-1.6: Mechanisms in place to inventory AI systems, resourced according to organizational risk priorities.
GOVERN-1.7: Processes and procedures for decommissioning and phasing out AI systems safely without increasing risks.
GOVERN-2.1: Roles, responsibilities, and lines of communication related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks documented and
GOVERN-2.2: Personnel and partners receive AI risk management training to perform duties consistent with policies and procedures.
GOVERN-2.3: Executive leadership takes responsibility for decisions about risks associated with AI system development and deployment
GOVERN-3.1: Decision-making informed by diverse team reflecting demographic, disciplinary, and experiential diversity.
GOVERN-3.2: Policies and procedures define and differentiate roles for human-AI configurations and system oversight.
GOVERN-4.1: Organizational policies and practices foster critical thinking and safety-first mindset in AI design, development, deplo
GOVERN-4.2: Teams document risks and potential impacts of AI technology; communicate impacts broadly.
GOVERN-4.3: Organizational practices enable AI testing, incident identification, and information sharing.
GOVERN-5.1: Policies and practices in place to collect, consider, prioritize, and integrate external feedback regarding potential in
GOVERN-5.2: Mechanisms established to regularly incorporate adjudicated feedback from relevant AI actors into system design and impl
GOVERN-6.1: Policies and procedures address AI risks associated with third-party entities, including intellectual property infringem
GOVERN-6.2: Contingency processes in place to handle failures or incidents in high-risk third-party data or AI systems.
MANAGE-1.1: Determination made whether AI system achieves intended purpose and stated objectives; whether development or deployment
MANAGE-1.2: Treatment of documented AI risks prioritized based on impact, likelihood, or available resources or methods.
MANAGE-1.3: Responses to high-priority risks developed, planned, and documented (mitigate, transfer, avoid, or accept).
MANAGE-1.4: Negative residual risks to downstream acquirers and end users documented.
MANAGE-2.1: Resources required to manage AI risks taken into account, along with viable non-AI alternatives.
MANAGE-2.2: Mechanisms in place and applied to sustain value of deployed AI systems.
MANAGE-2.3: Procedures followed to respond to and recover from previously unknown risk when identified.
MANAGE-2.4: Mechanisms in place and responsibilities assigned to supersede, disengage, or deactivate AI systems demonstrating incons
MANAGE-3.1: AI risks and benefits from third-party resources regularly monitored; risk controls applied and documented.
MANAGE-3.2: Pre-trained models used for development monitored as part of regular AI system monitoring and maintenance.
MANAGE-4.1: Post-deployment monitoring plans implemented, including mechanisms for capturing end-user input, appeal and override, de
MANAGE-4.2: Measurable activities for continual improvements integrated into AI system updates; regular engagement with interested p
MANAGE-4.3: Incidents and errors communicated to relevant AI actors including affected communities; processes for tracking, respondi
MAP-1.1: Intended purpose, beneficial uses, context-specific laws, norms, expectations, and deployment settings understood and do
MAP-1.2: Interdisciplinary AI actors with diverse competencies and documentation of their participation.
MAP-1.3: Organization's mission and relevant goals for the AI technology understood and documented.
MAP-1.4: Business value or context of business use clearly defined or re-evaluated.
MAP-1.5: Organizational risk tolerances determined and documented.
MAP-1.6: System requirements elicited from and understood by relevant AI actors; design decisions consider socio-technical implic
MAP-2.1: Specific task and implementation methods that the AI system will support are defined.
MAP-2.2: Information about AI system's knowledge limits and how output may be utilized and overseen by humans documented.
MAP-2.3: Scientific integrity and Test, Evaluation, Validation, and Verification (TEVV) considerations identified and documented.
MAP-3.1: Potential benefits of intended AI system functionality and performance examined and documented.
MAP-3.2: Potential costs (including non-monetary) resulting from expected or realized AI errors examined and documented.
MAP-3.3: Targeted application scope specified and documented based on system capability and established context.
MAP-3.4: Processes for operator and practitioner proficiency with AI system performance defined, assessed, and documented.
MAP-3.5: Processes for human oversight defined, assessed, and documented in accordance with organizational policies.
MAP-4.1: Approaches for mapping AI technology and legal risks of components in place, followed, and documented, including third-p
MAP-4.2: Internal risk controls for AI system components, including third-party technologies, identified and documented.
MAP-5.1: Likelihood and magnitude of each identified impact examined and documented based on expected use, similar contexts, publ
MAP-5.2: Practices and personnel supporting regular engagement with relevant AI actors and integrating feedback about positive, n

MEASURE-1.2: Appropriateness of AI metrics and effectiveness of existing controls regularly assessed and updated, including error rep

MEASURE-2.1: Test sets, metrics, and tool details used during TEVV documented.
MEASURE-2.10: Privacy risk of the AI system examined and documented.
MEASURE-2.11: Fairness and bias evaluated; results documented.
MEASURE-2.12: Environmental impact and sustainability of AI model training and management assessed and documented.
MEASURE-2.13: Effectiveness of employed TEVV metrics and processes evaluated and documented.
MEASURE-2.2: Evaluations involving human subjects meet applicable requirements and are representative of relevant population.

MEASURE-2.6: AI system regularly evaluated for safety risks; demonstrated to be safe with residual risk not exceeding tolerance; can
MEASURE-2.7: AI system security and resilience evaluated and documented.
MEASURE-2.8: Risks associated with transparency and accountability examined and documented.
MEASURE-2.9: AI model explained, validated, and documented; output interpreted within context to inform responsible use and governanc
MEASURE-3.1: Approaches, personnel, and documentation in place to regularly identify and track existing, unanticipated, and emergent
MEASURE-3.2: Risk tracking approaches considered for settings where AI risks are difficult to assess using current measurement techni
MEASURE-3.3: Feedback processes for end users and impacted communities to report problems and appeal outcomes established and integra
MEASURE-4.1: Measurement approaches for identifying AI risks connected to deployment context(s) and informed through consultation wit

MEASURE-4.3: Measurable performance improvements or declines identified and documented based on relevant actor consultations and fiel
